title: PRINCIPLES FOR THE USE OF SAMPLING AND TESTING IN INTERNATIONAL FOOD TRADE CAC/GL 83-2013

sections.section: PRINCIPLES FOR THE USE OF SAMPLING AND TESTING IN INTERNATIONAL FOOD TRADE CAC/GL 83-2013
sections.summary: Document title: Principles for the Use of Sampling and Testing in International Food Trade (CAC/GL 83‑2013).

Explains that sampling and testing are tools to assess whether traded foods meet specifications and must be scientifically based to control probabilities of wrongful acceptance/rejection, with Codex sampling and endorsed analytical methods preferred; it also says procedures should support risk‑based decisions within national food control systems and complement other Codex texts (e.g., CAC/GL 47‑2003, CAC/GL 62‑2007).

States the principles are intended to help governments establish and use scientifically based sampling and testing procedures for international food trade compliance, and that explanatory notes clarify their application.
Defines 'Testing' as the process to examine the specified characteristics of a sample.
Operational heading describing requirements for testing procedures, including sample preparation and analytical methods to determine the characteristics of a sample.

sections.section: ## Principle 4: Selecting appropriate sampling and testing procedures
sections.summary: Sampling and testing procedures must be scientifically based, appropriate to the commodity and lot/consignment, fit for their intended purpose and applied consistently, taking practical matters (cost, timeliness, access) and within-lot variation into account; attributes plans are recommended for qualitative or unknown distributions and variables plans for normally distributed measurements, with guidance to consult CAC/GL 50-2004 and relevant ISO standards and to use random sampling where possible, while noting CAC/GL 50 does not cover non-homogeneous lots.

Product assessment procedures must take analytical measurement uncertainty into account; uncertainty covers all steps from sampling to determination, exporting and importing countries should agree how to treat uncertainty when assessing conformity against legal limits, and Codex guidelines (e.g. CAC/GL 54-2004, CAC/GL 59-2006) describe estimation and decision examples.

Sampling and testing are 'fit for purpose' when, with appropriate decision criteria, they yield acceptable probabilities of wrong acceptance or rejection; fitness requires balancing consumer and producer risk and laboratories should meet competence and QA criteria (ISO/IEC 17025, proficiency testing, validated methods, internal quality control) and follow Codex laboratory guidance.
Sampling and testing procedures should be reviewed periodically to incorporate new science and information.
